for (m in seq_len(n_markers_sma)) {

  marker_geno <- geno_sma[, m]
  df_temp     <- data.frame(PH = pheno_sma, G = marker_geno)
  df_temp     <- df_temp[complete.cases(df_temp), ]

  if (nrow(df_temp) < 10) next

  fit <- summary(lm(PH ~ G, data = df_temp))
  n_i <- nrow(df_temp)

  LOD <- (n_i / 2) * log10(
    fit$fstatistic[1] * (1 / (n_i - 1 - 1)) + 1
  )

  res_sma[m, ] <- c(
    fit$coefficients[1, 1],
    fit$coefficients[2, 1],
    fit$coefficients[2, 3],
    fit$coefficients[2, 4],
    fit$fstatistic[1],
    LOD,
    fit$r.squared
  )
}

res_sma <- res_sma[complete.cases(res_sma), ]

bonf_p   <- 0.05 / nrow(res_sma)
bonf_lod <- min(
  res_sma[res_sma[, "P value"] <= bonf_p, "LOD score"],
  na.rm = TRUE
)

cat("Bonferroni P threshold:", signif(bonf_p, 3), "\n")
## Bonferroni P threshold: 0.000207
cat("Corresponding LOD threshold:", round(bonf_lod, 3), "\n\n")
## Corresponding LOD threshold: 3.114
sig_markers <- res_sma[res_sma[, "P value"] < bonf_p, , drop = FALSE]
cat("Significant markers after Bonferroni correction:",
    nrow(sig_markers), "\n\n")
## Significant markers after Bonferroni correction: 13
if (nrow(sig_markers) > 0) {
  cat("Top significant markers (sorted by LOD score):\n")
  print(
    round(
      sig_markers[order(sig_markers[, "LOD score"],
                        decreasing = TRUE), ],
      4
    )
  )
}
## Top significant markers (sorted by LOD score):
##             Intercept Effect(cm) t value P value F value LOD score R-squared
## PZA01142.4   142.5240   -12.8727 -5.7361   0e+00 32.9027    6.6218    0.1583
## PHM532.23    142.4955   -12.3229 -5.5078   0e+00 30.3353    6.1419    0.1485
## PZA02513.1   141.8517   -11.5041 -5.1310   0e+00 26.3277    5.3916    0.1289
## PZA01470.1   142.1524   -10.8471 -4.8882   0e+00 23.8941    4.9247    0.1178
## PZA02683.1   141.9085   -10.4062 -4.6419   0e+00 21.5477    4.4664    0.1080
## PZA02386.2   140.2764   -12.6625 -4.6674   0e+00 21.7843    4.4447    0.1417
## PZA03172.3   141.5014   -10.4114 -4.5582   0e+00 20.7776    4.3121    0.1061
## PHM2350.17   141.9987   -10.1638 -4.4656   0e+00 19.9417    4.1467    0.1028
## PHM3978.104  140.9754    -9.6508 -4.3384   0e+00 18.8221    3.9297    0.0951
## PZA00395.2   140.7347    -9.6157 -4.1939   0e+00 17.5891    3.6818    0.0909
## PZA03717.1   140.8153    -9.2976 -4.0687   1e-04 16.5539    3.4752    0.0855
## PZA02019.1   141.0201    -9.2692 -4.0671   1e-04 16.5414    3.4733    0.0850
## PZA02941.7   132.5425     8.9677  3.8411   2e-04 14.7543    3.1145    0.0750
save(res_sma, bonf_lod, sig_markers,
     file = "data/sma_results.RData")
cat("\nSMA results saved to data/sma_results.RData\n")
## 
## SMA results saved to data/sma_results.RData
# plotting LOD by cumulative map position across all 10 LGs, with
# chromosome boundaries marked - lets the whole genome scan be read
# as one continuous figure rather than 10 separate ones

chr_map   <- pull.map(z020)
lod_df    <- data.frame(
  marker   = rownames(res_sma),
  LOD      = res_sma[, "LOD score"],
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

lod_df$chr <- NA
lod_df$pos <- NA

for (ch in seq_along(chr_map)) {
  mrk_names <- names(chr_map[[ch]])
  idx       <- lod_df$marker %in% mrk_names
  lod_df$chr[idx] <- ch
  lod_df$pos[idx] <- chr_map[[ch]][
    match(lod_df$marker[idx], mrk_names)
  ]
}

chr_lengths  <- sapply(chr_map, max)
chr_offsets  <- c(0, cumsum(chr_lengths[-length(chr_lengths)]))
lod_df$cum_pos <- lod_df$pos + chr_offsets[lod_df$chr]

lod_df <- lod_df[order(lod_df$cum_pos), ]

plot(
  lod_df$cum_pos, lod_df$LOD,
  type  = "h",
  col   = "steelblue",
  lwd   = 1.2,
  xlab  = "Map position (cM)",
  ylab  = "LOD score",
  main  = "Single Marker Analysis -- Plant Height",
  xaxt  = "n",
  ylim  = c(0, max(lod_df$LOD, bonf_lod * 1.2, na.rm = TRUE))
)

for (ch in seq_along(chr_map)) {
  mid_pos <- chr_offsets[ch] + chr_lengths[ch] / 2
  axis(1, at = mid_pos, labels = paste0("LG", ch), las = 2, cex.axis = 0.7)
  if (ch < length(chr_map)) {
    abline(v = chr_offsets[ch] + chr_lengths[ch],
           col = "grey70", lty = 2, lwd = 0.8)
  }
}

abline(h = bonf_lod, col = "firebrick", lty = 2, lwd = 1.5)
text(
  x      = max(lod_df$cum_pos, na.rm = TRUE) * 0.02,
  y      = bonf_lod + max(lod_df$LOD, na.rm = TRUE) * 0.03,
  labels = paste0("Threshold (LOD = ", round(bonf_lod, 2), ")"),
  col    = "firebrick",
  cex    = 0.75,
  adj    = 0
)

Figure 10. Single marker analysis LOD scores across all 10 linkage groups, in map order. The dashed red line marks the Bonferroni significance threshold. 

# cross-checking the manual lm() loop above against R/qtl's own
# marker regression (scanone method = "mr") - if these two
# independent routes agree, that's good confidence the manual loop
# and the LOD conversion formula are both correct

z020.mr <- scanone(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  method    = "mr"
)

cat("scanone() marker regression complete.\n\n")
## scanone() marker regression complete.
cat("Markers with LOD above Bonferroni threshold (",
    round(bonf_lod, 3), "):\n")
## Markers with LOD above Bonferroni threshold ( 3.114 ):
print(summary(z020.mr, threshold = bonf_lod))
##            chr pos  lod
## PZA01470.1   4  67 4.92
## PZA01142.4   6 109 6.62
## PZA02386.2  10 101 4.44
save(z020.mr, file = "data/z020_mr.RData")

plot(
  z020.mr,
  ylab = "LOD score",
  main = "Single Marker Analysis (scanone) -- Plant Height",
  type = "p",
  col  = "steelblue"
)
abline(h = bonf_lod, col = "firebrick", lty = 2, lwd = 1.5)

# pulling out the top SMA marker for a closer look, plotPXG() shows
# the phenotype-by-genotype distribution directly

top_marker <- rownames(z020.mr)[which.max(z020.mr$lod)]

cat("Top marker from SMA:", top_marker, "\n")
## Top marker from SMA: PZA01142.4
cat("LOD score:", round(max(z020.mr$lod, na.rm = TRUE), 3), "\n")
## LOD score: 6.622
cat("Chromosome:", z020.mr$chr[which.max(z020.mr$lod)], "\n")
## Chromosome: 6
cat("Position (cM):", round(z020.mr$pos[which.max(z020.mr$lod)], 2), "\n\n")
## Position (cM): 109.39
if (top_marker %in% rownames(res_sma)) {
  cat("Additive effect (BB - AA):",
      round(res_sma[top_marker, "Effect(cm)"], 3), "cm\n")
  cat("R-squared:",
      round(res_sma[top_marker, "R-squared"], 4), "\n")
  cat("Variance explained:",
      round(res_sma[top_marker, "R-squared"] * 100, 2), "%\n")
}
## Additive effect (BB - AA): -12.873 cm
## R-squared: 0.1583 
## Variance explained: 15.83 %
plotPXG(z020, top_marker, pheno.col = "PlantHeight")

# fitqtl() at the top SMA marker, using multiple imputation
# (n.draws = 32) for a proper effect size estimate rather than just
# trusting the nearest-marker lm() coefficient
#
# step = 0 here since this is a marker-position-only fit, not a
# between-marker interval estimate

z020 <- sim.geno(z020, n.draws = 32, step = 0)

top_chr <- z020.mr$chr[which.max(z020.mr$lod)]
top_pos <- z020.mr$pos[which.max(z020.mr$lod)]

cat("Fitting QTL at:\n")
## Fitting QTL at:
cat("  Marker    :", top_marker, "\n")
##   Marker    : PZA01142.4
cat("  Chromosome:", top_chr, "\n")
##   Chromosome: 6
cat("  Position  :", round(top_pos, 2), "cM\n\n")
##   Position  : 109.39 cM
z020.qtl <- makeqtl(z020, chr = top_chr, pos = top_pos)

z020.fit <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  qtl     = z020.qtl,
  formula = y ~ Q1,
  get.ests = TRUE,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight"
)

print(summary(z020.fit))
## 
##      fitqtl summary
## 
## Method: multiple imputation 
## Model:  normal phenotype
## Number of observations : 188 
## 
## Full model result
## ----------------------------------  
## Model formula: y ~ Q1 
## 
##        df       SS        MS      LOD     %var Pvalue(Chi2)    Pvalue(F)
## Model   1  7389.23 7389.2295 6.779321 15.30075 2.303892e-08 2.851509e-08
## Error 186 40904.02  219.9141                                            
## Total 187 48293.25                                                      
## 
## 
## Estimated effects:
## -----------------
##               est      SE       t
## Intercept 136.465   1.085 125.733
## 6@109.4    -6.360   1.093  -5.817
save(z020.qtl, z020.fit, top_marker, top_chr, top_pos,
     file = "data/sma_fitqtl.RData")
cat("\nfitqtl results saved to data/sma_fitqtl.RData\n")
## 
## fitqtl results saved to data/sma_fitqtl.RData

8.6 Single Marker Analysis Results Summary

Single marker analysis tested all 242 markers in the final linkage map for association with plant height, identifying 13 markers significant after Bonferroni correction. The strongest signal, on linkage group 6, explained nearly 16% of phenotypic variance on its own, a substantial proportion for a single marker and an early indication that this region carries a major QTL for plant height. Secondary signals on linkage groups 4 and 5 were weaker but consistent with the pattern later confirmed by more sophisticated mapping methods. The one unexpected result was the antagonistic marker on linkage group 10, where the B73 allele rather than NC350 was associated with greater height, a finding that warranted closer attention in later sections. The absence of any significant markers on six of the ten chromosomes pointed towards a moderate-effect genetic architecture rather than a highly polygenic one, a pattern that held up throughout the rest of the analysis.

8.7 Interval Mapping

# IM needs genotype probabilities at every position along the map,
# not just at the markers themselves - calc.genoprob() does this
# using the flanking markers and the Kosambi distances already in
# z020
#
# step = 1 -> a probability calculated every 1 cM, on top of the
# marker positions, ~1550 testing positions across the ~1549 cM map
#
# riself means only 2 QTL genotype classes possible at any position
# (NC350/NC350 or B73/B73) - simpler than the backcross/F2 cases with
# 2-4 classes

z020 <- calc.genoprob(z020, step = 1)

cat("Genotype probabilities calculated.\n")
## Genotype probabilities calculated.
cat("Step size: 1 cM\n")
## Step size: 1 cM
cat("Number of chromosomes:", nchr(z020), "\n")
## Number of chromosomes: 10
cat("Total map length (cM):", round(sum(sapply(pull.map(z020), max)), 1), "\n")
## Total map length (cM): 1548.7
# scanone() at every genoprob position, fitting plant height against
# QTL genotype prob and recording LOD - running both:
#
#   em : exact ML via Expectation-Maximization, slower, the gold
#        standard hk is approximating
#   hk : Haley-Knott regression, fast least-squares approximation,
#        holds up well with moderate missing data and decent marker
#        spacing
#
# if em and hk profiles match closely, that's good evidence the
# result isn't an artefact of the estimation method chosen

z020.em <- scanone(z020, pheno.col = "PlantHeight", method = "em")
cat("EM scan complete.\n")
## EM scan complete.
z020.hk <- scanone(z020, pheno.col = "PlantHeight", method = "hk")
cat("HK scan complete.\n\n")
## HK scan complete.
cat("--- Top position, EM method ---\n")
## --- Top position, EM method ---
print(z020.em[which.max(z020.em$lod), ])
##           chr pos      lod
## c6.loc108   6 108 7.337647
cat("\n--- Top position, HK method ---\n")
## 
## --- Top position, HK method ---
print(z020.hk[which.max(z020.hk$lod), ])
##           chr pos      lod
## c6.loc108   6 108 7.377878
save(z020.em, z020.hk, file = "data/im_scanone.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/im_scanone.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/im_scanone.RData
# permutation test for the genome-wide significance threshold -
# shuffle PlantHeight across individuals 1000 times, breaking the
# real genotype-phenotype link, keep the single highest LOD from each
# shuffled scan. 95th percentile of those 1000 max LODs = alpha 0.05
# threshold
#
# running both methods to match what gets compared later:
#   hk : fast, runs directly
#   em : slow on a 1549 cM map x 1000 perms, cached to disk so a
#        session restart doesn't mean starting over
#
# set.seed() so both runs are reproducible if re-run

set.seed(678)
z020.perm.hk <- scanone(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  method    = "hk",
  n.perm    = 1000,
  verbose   = FALSE
)
cat("HK permutations complete.\n")
## HK permutations complete.
rdata_perm_em <- "data/im_perm_em.RData"

if (file.exists(rdata_perm_em)) {

  load(rdata_perm_em)
  cat("Loaded cached EM permutation results from:", rdata_perm_em, "\n")

} else {

  cat("Running 1000 EM permutations. This will take a while.\n")
  cat("R will save the result automatically when done.\n")

  set.seed(678)
  z020.perm.em <- scanone(
    z020,
    pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
    method    = "em",
    n.perm    = 1000,
    verbose   = FALSE
  )

  save(z020.perm.em, file = rdata_perm_em)
  cat("EM permutations complete. Saved to:", rdata_perm_em, "\n")
}
## Loaded cached EM permutation results from: data/im_perm_em.RData
cat("\n--- LOD threshold at alpha = 0.05 ---\n")
## 
## --- LOD threshold at alpha = 0.05 ---
cat("HK method:\n")
## HK method:
print(summary(z020.perm.hk, alpha = 0.05))
## LOD thresholds (1000 permutations)
##     lod
## 5% 2.79
cat("EM method:\n")
## EM method:
print(summary(z020.perm.em, alpha = 0.05))
## LOD thresholds (1000 permutations)
##     lod
## 5% 2.99
save(z020.perm.hk, z020.perm.em,
     file = "data/im_perm_both.RData")
cat("\nBoth permutation objects saved to data/im_perm_both.RData\n")
## 
## Both permutation objects saved to data/im_perm_both.RData
# summary() with perms= returns only positions clearing the
# permutation threshold at the chosen alpha - one row per peak
#
# note: summary() on a scanone perm object returns a named numeric
# vector, so pulling the threshold needs ["lod"], not $lod

thres_em <- summary(z020.perm.em, alpha = 0.05)["lod"]
thres_hk <- summary(z020.perm.hk, alpha = 0.05)["lod"]

cat("=== Significant QTL positions from Interval Mapping ===\n\n")
## === Significant QTL positions from Interval Mapping ===
cat("--- EM method (threshold LOD =", round(thres_em, 2), ") ---\n")
## --- EM method (threshold LOD = NA ) ---
print(summary(z020.em, perms = z020.perm.em, alpha = 0.05))
##            chr   pos  lod
## c4.loc67     4  67.0 4.75
## PZA02941.7   5  14.9 3.20
## c6.loc108    6 108.0 7.34
## c10.loc108  10 108.0 3.29
cat("\n--- HK method (threshold LOD =", round(thres_hk, 2), ") ---\n")
## 
## --- HK method (threshold LOD = NA ) ---
print(summary(z020.hk, perms = z020.perm.hk, alpha = 0.05))
##            chr   pos  lod
## c4.loc67     4  67.0 4.61
## PZA02941.7   5  14.9 3.18
## c6.loc108    6 108.0 7.38
## c10.loc107  10 107.0 3.28
# overlaying em and hk LOD profiles plus their thresholds - should
# sit almost on top of each other, any visible gap is where missing
# genotype data makes the hk approximation drift slightly from the
# exact em solution

plot(
  z020.em, z020.hk,
  ylab  = "LOD score",
  main  = "Interval Mapping -- Plant Height",
  col   = c("red", "blue"),
  lwd   = 2
)

add.threshold(z020.em, perms = z020.perm.em, col = "red",   lty = 2)
add.threshold(z020.hk, perms = z020.perm.hk, col = "blue",  lty = 2)

legend(
  "topright",
  legend  = c("EM", "HK"),
  lty     = 1,
  lwd     = 2,
  col     = c("red", "blue"),
  box.col = NA
)

Figure 11. Interval mapping LOD profiles across the genome, comparing the EM (red) and HK (blue) methods. Dashed lines mark each method’s genome-wide significance threshold. 

# fitqtl() at the IM peak (LG6, 108 cM), using multiple imputation
# so the effect estimate is attributed to the inferred QTL position
# rather than whichever marker happens to be nearest
#
# re-running sim.geno() with step = 1 here to match the IM grid - the
# earlier sim.geno() call used step = 0 for SMA and that draw set
# doesn't apply to interval positions
#
# riself -> fitqtl() fits 2 classes (NC350/NC350 vs B73/B73), and the
# additive effect it reports is HALF the homozygote difference -
# different convention to the lm() effect from SMA, which is the
# FULL difference. multiply fitqtl's estimate by 2 to compare the two

z020 <- sim.geno(z020, n.draws = 32, step = 1)

im_top_chr <- 6
im_top_pos <- 108

z020.im.qtl <- makeqtl(z020, chr = im_top_chr, pos = im_top_pos)

cat("QTL object built at:\n")
## QTL object built at:
cat("  Chromosome:", im_top_chr, "\n")
##   Chromosome: 6
cat("  Position  :", im_top_pos, "cM\n\n")
##   Position  : 108 cM
print(z020.im.qtl)
##   QTL object containing imputed genotypes, with 32 imputations. 
## 
##       name chr pos n.gen
## Q1 6@108.0   6 108     2
z020.im.fit <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  qtl       = z020.im.qtl,
  formula   = y ~ Q1,
  get.ests  = TRUE,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight"
)

print(summary(z020.im.fit))
## 
##      fitqtl summary
## 
## Method: multiple imputation 
## Model:  normal phenotype
## Number of observations : 188 
## 
## Full model result
## ----------------------------------  
## Model formula: y ~ Q1 
## 
##        df       SS        MS     LOD     %var Pvalue(Chi2)    Pvalue(F)
## Model   1  7769.56 7769.5604 7.16068 16.08829 9.329634e-09 1.168222e-08
## Error 186 40523.69  217.8693                                           
## Total 187 48293.25                                                     
## 
## 
## Estimated effects:
## -----------------
##               est      SE       t
## Intercept 136.549   1.088 125.550
## 6@108.0    -6.494   1.091  -5.953
save(z020.im.qtl, z020.im.fit,
     file = "data/im_fitqtl.RData")
cat("\nfitqtl results saved to data/im_fitqtl.RData\n")
## 
## fitqtl results saved to data/im_fitqtl.RData

8.8 Interval Mapping Results Summary

Interval mapping confirmed the linkage group 6 signal as the dominant QTL for plant height, with its LOD score rising from 6.62 under single marker analysis to 7.34, reflecting the added resolution gained from testing positions between markers rather than only at marker locations. Three further regions on linkage groups 4, 5, and 10 cleared the genome-wide significance threshold, matching the candidate regions flagged by single marker analysis. The close agreement between the EM and HK estimation methods across the whole genome scan gave confidence that these results were not sensitive to the choice of algorithm. Taken together with the single marker analysis, this stage of the project confirmed four candidate QTL regions worth carrying forward into composite interval mapping, where background genetic variation could be properly controlled for.

8.9 Reload Point: Restoring Objects for a New Session

# run this at the start of any new R session to bring back every
# object needed for the rest of the report, without rerunning any of
# the slow computation (two-point rf, marker ordering, permutations)

library(onemap)
library(qtl)

raw_data <- read.csv(
  file      = "data/Z020 NC350 x B73.csv",
  header    = TRUE,
  row.names = 1
)

is_marker_col <- function(x) {
  non_missing <- x[!is.na(x)]
  all(non_missing %in% c(0, 1, 2))
}
marker_flags <- sapply(raw_data, is_marker_col)
marker_cols  <- names(raw_data)[marker_flags]
geno_mat     <- as.matrix(raw_data[, marker_cols])

ph_df        <- read.csv("data/plant_height.csv")
plant_height <- ph_df$PlantHeight
names(plant_height) <- ph_df$Ind

load("data/my_data.RData")    # my_data
load("data/bc_twopts.RData")  # bc_twopts
load("data/bc_map.RData")     # bc_map, bc_ord_safe

z020 <- read.cross(
  format    = "mm",
  file      = "data/Z020_qtl_filtered.raw",
  mapfile   = "data/bc.map",
  crosstype = "riself"
)
##  --Read the following data:
##  Type of cross:          bc 
##  Number of individuals:  188 
##  Number of markers:      242 
##  Number of phenotypes:   0 
##  --Cross type: riself
z020$pheno$PlantHeight <- plant_height[rownames(raw_data)]

z020 <- calc.genoprob(z020, step = 1)
z020 <- sim.geno(z020, n.draws = 32, step = 1)

load("data/sma_results.RData")   # res_sma, bonf_lod, sig_markers
load("data/z020_mr.RData")       # z020.mr
load("data/sma_fitqtl.RData")    # z020.qtl, z020.fit, top_marker, top_chr, top_pos

load("data/im_scanone.RData")    # z020.em, z020.hk
load("data/im_perm_both.RData")  # z020.perm.em, z020.perm.hk
load("data/im_fitqtl.RData")     # z020.im.qtl, z020.im.fit

cat("All objects restored successfully.\n")
## All objects restored successfully.
cat("bc_map groups:", length(bc_map), "\n")
## bc_map groups: 10
cat("z020 individuals:", nrow(z020$pheno), "\n")
## z020 individuals: 188
cat("Total markers in map:", sum(sapply(pull.map(z020), length)), "\n")
## Total markers in map: 242

8.10 Composite Interval Mapping

# cim() lives in qtl, already loaded - just confirming the objects
# needed from interval mapping are in the environment before going
# further. if starting fresh, run reload-saved-objects above first

library(qtl)

stopifnot(exists("z020"))
stopifnot(exists("z020.em"))
stopifnot(exists("z020.perm.em"))

cat("z020 cross object present:", exists("z020"), "\n")
## z020 cross object present: TRUE
cat("Individuals:", nind(z020), "\n")
## Individuals: 188
cat("Total markers:", totmar(z020), "\n")
## Total markers: 242
cat("Chromosomes:", nchr(z020), "\n")
## Chromosomes: 10
cat("Genotype probabilities calculated:",
    "prob" %in% names(z020$geno[[1]]), "\n")
## Genotype probabilities calculated: TRUE
cat("\nReady to proceed with Composite Interval Mapping.\n")
## 
## Ready to proceed with Composite Interval Mapping.
# cim() tests plant height against the QTL genotype probability at
# each position, plus a set of marker cofactors elsewhere in the
# genome that soak up the effect of other QTL so they don't blur the
# test at the current position
#
# n.marcovar = 27, following 2*sqrt(N) for N = 188 - matches the
# professor's value exactly since our population is the same size
#
# window = 15: cofactors within 15 cM of the tested position get
# excluded, so a cofactor can't accidentally absorb the very QTL
# being tested
#
# method = "hk" - cim() is heavier than plain scanone, hk keeps it
# practical
#
# running this straight on z020, no convert2riself() needed since
# ours was built as riself from the start (the professor's tutorial
# converts because his file was read in as a backcross)

set.seed(678)
z020.cim15 <- cim(
  cross      = z020,
  pheno.col  = "PlantHeight",
  method     = "hk",
  n.marcovar = 27,
  window     = 15
)

cat("CIM with window = 15 cM complete.\n\n")
## CIM with window = 15 cM complete.
cat("Peak LOD per chromosome:\n")
## Peak LOD per chromosome:
print(summary(z020.cim15))
##              chr   pos   lod
## PHM4204.69     1 107.8  1.98
## c2.loc15       2  15.0  2.36
## c3.loc196      3 196.0  3.69
## c4.loc66       4  66.0 10.18
## c5.loc10       5  10.0  2.97
## c6.loc102      6 102.0 15.29
## PZA03063.21    7  58.6  1.53
## PZA02381.1     8 126.5  7.57
## c9.loc122      9 122.0  7.38
## PZA01909.1.2  10 173.1  4.54
save(z020.cim15, file = "data/cim_window15.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/cim_window15.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/cim_window15.RData
# same cim() run but window = Inf - the strictest setting, no
# cofactor allowed anywhere on the SAME linkage group as the tested
# position, only cofactors from other chromosomes
#
# useful as a check against the window = 15 result: a peak holding up
# under window = Inf is good evidence it's real and not an artefact
# of a nearby cofactor distorting the local model. a peak that
# shrinks a lot under window = Inf needs more caution

set.seed(678)
z020.cimInf <- cim(
  cross      = z020,
  pheno.col  = "PlantHeight",
  method     = "hk",
  n.marcovar = 27,
  window     = Inf
)

cat("CIM with window = Inf complete.\n\n")
## CIM with window = Inf complete.
cat("Peak LOD per chromosome:\n")
## Peak LOD per chromosome:
print(summary(z020.cimInf))
##             chr   pos   lod
## c1.loc109     1 109.0  2.59
## c2.loc15      2  15.0  2.36
## c3.loc195     3 195.0  3.86
## c4.loc66      4  66.0 14.58
## c5.loc10      5  10.0  1.82
## c6.loc108     6 108.0 18.58
## PZA03063.21   7  58.6  1.53
## PZA02381.1    8 126.5  6.95
## PZA01267.3    9 122.1  4.33
## c10.loc171   10 171.0  5.78
save(z020.cimInf, file = "data/cim_windowInf.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/cim_windowInf.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/cim_windowInf.RData
# same permutation logic as IM, just on cim() now - shuffle
# PlantHeight 1000 times, record the max LOD per shuffle, 95th
# percentile = alpha 0.05 threshold
#
# running the permutation at window = Inf only: it's the most
# conservative setting so the resulting threshold is valid across
# both window sizes, and running just one permutation set instead of
# two saves a lot of time given this is the slowest step in the CIM
# section
#
# cached to disk, this one's slow enough that a session restart
# shouldn't mean starting over

rdata_perm_cim <- "data/cim_perm.RData"

if (file.exists(rdata_perm_cim)) {

  load(rdata_perm_cim)
  cat("Loaded cached CIM permutation results from:", rdata_perm_cim, "\n")

} else {

  cat("Running 1000 CIM permutations with window = Inf.\n")
  cat("This is the slowest step in the CIM section. Please wait.\n")

  set.seed(678)
  z020.perm.cim <- cim(
    cross      = z020,
    pheno.col  = "PlantHeight",
    method     = "hk",
    n.marcovar = 27,
    window     = Inf,
    n.perm     = 1000
  )

  save(z020.perm.cim, file = rdata_perm_cim)
  cat("CIM permutations complete. Saved to:", rdata_perm_cim, "\n")
}
## Loaded cached CIM permutation results from: data/cim_perm.RData
cim_threshold <- summary(z020.perm.cim, alpha = 0.05)
cat("\n--- CIM LOD threshold at alpha = 0.05 ---\n")
## 
## --- CIM LOD threshold at alpha = 0.05 ---
print(cim_threshold)
## LOD thresholds (1000 permutations)
##    [,1]
## 5% 6.24
# applying the LOD 6.24 threshold to both window scans to see which
# peaks are genuinely significant rather than just locally tall
#
# summary() with perms= only returns positions clearing the
# threshold, max one row per chromosome (the highest peak that
# clears it)

cat("=== Significant QTL from CIM, window = 15 ===\n")
## === Significant QTL from CIM, window = 15 ===
cim15_sig <- summary(z020.cim15, perms = z020.perm.cim, alpha = 0.05)
print(cim15_sig)
##            chr pos   lod
## c4.loc66     4  66 10.18
## c6.loc102    6 102 15.29
## PZA02381.1   8 127  7.57
## c9.loc122    9 122  7.38
cat("\n=== Significant QTL from CIM, window = Inf ===\n")
## 
## === Significant QTL from CIM, window = Inf ===
cimInf_sig <- summary(z020.cimInf, perms = z020.perm.cim, alpha = 0.05)
print(cimInf_sig)
##            chr pos   lod
## c4.loc66     4  66 14.58
## c6.loc108    6 108 18.58
## PZA02381.1   8 127  6.95
save(cim15_sig, cimInf_sig, cim_threshold,
     file = "data/cim_significant.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/cim_significant.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/cim_significant.RData
# both CIM window scans overlaid, threshold drawn as a horizontal
# line, green ticks via add.cim.covar() showing where the window = 15
# cofactors were picked - comparing window settings visually makes it
# obvious which peaks are stable vs sensitive to the window choice

plot(
  z020.cim15, z020.cimInf,
  col  = c("orange", "cyan"),
  main = "Composite Interval Mapping -- Plant Height"
)

add.threshold(
  z020.cim15,
  perms = z020.perm.cim,
  alpha = 0.05,
  col   = "firebrick",
  lty   = 2
)

add.cim.covar(z020.cim15, col = "darkgreen")

legend(
  "topright",
  legend = c("window = 15", "window = Inf", "Threshold (LOD 6.24)"),
  lty    = c(1, 1, 2),
  col    = c("orange", "cyan", "firebrick"),
  box.col = NA
)

Figure 12. Composite interval mapping LOD profiles across all 10 linkage groups, comparing window = 15 cM (orange) and window = infinite (cyan) cofactor exclusion settings. The dashed red line marks the genome-wide significance threshold (LOD 6.24, alpha = 0.05). Green ticks mark positions of marker cofactors selected for the window = 15 scan. 

# building the QTL object from the 3 CIM-confirmed positions (LG4,
# LG6, LG8), using the window = Inf positions since that's the
# stricter scan and its peaks are a touch more stable (no
# same-chromosome cofactor can nudge them)
#
# sim.geno() re-run here so the imputation draws match the current
# genoprob grid - same n.draws = 32, step = 1 as the IM fitqtl step

z020 <- sim.geno(z020, n.draws = 32, step = 1)

cim_chr <- cimInf_sig$chr
cim_pos <- cimInf_sig$pos

cat("Building QTL object at:\n")
## Building QTL object at:
for (i in seq_along(cim_chr)) {
  cat("  Q", i, ": LG", cim_chr[i], " at ", round(cim_pos[i], 1),
      " cM (LOD ", round(cimInf_sig$lod[i], 2), ")\n", sep = "")
}
##   Q1: LG4 at 66 cM (LOD 14.58)
##   Q2: LG6 at 108 cM (LOD 18.58)
##   Q3: LG8 at 126.5 cM (LOD 6.95)
z020.cim.qtl <- makeqtl(z020, chr = cim_chr, pos = cim_pos)
print(z020.cim.qtl)
##   QTL object containing imputed genotypes, with 32 imputations. 
## 
##       name chr    pos n.gen
## Q1  4@66.0   4  66.00     2
## Q2 6@108.0   6 108.00     2
## Q3 8@126.5   8 126.53     2
save(z020.cim.qtl, cim_chr, cim_pos, file = "data/cim_qtl_object.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/cim_qtl_object.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/cim_qtl_object.RData
# fitting all 3 QTL together rather than one at a time:
#
#   - joint %var properly accounts for any shared variance between
#     QTL instead of double-counting overlap from 3 separate
#     single-QTL fits
#   - the drop-one-at-a-time table shows what each QTL contributes
#     ABOVE the other two, a fairer measure of independent
#     importance than a stand-alone effect
#
# formula = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3, additive only, no interaction term -
# epistasis testing is MIM's job later, not CIM's

z020.cim.fit <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  qtl       = z020.cim.qtl,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3,
  get.ests  = TRUE,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight"
)

cat("=== Combined three-QTL model (CIM) ===\n\n")
## === Combined three-QTL model (CIM) ===
print(summary(z020.cim.fit))
## 
##      fitqtl summary
## 
## Method: multiple imputation 
## Model:  normal phenotype
## Number of observations : 188 
## 
## Full model result
## ----------------------------------  
## Model formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 
## 
##        df       SS        MS      LOD     %var Pvalue(Chi2)    Pvalue(F)
## Model   3 16250.52 5416.8409 16.74675 33.64967 1.110223e-16 2.220446e-16
## Error 184 32042.73  174.1453                                            
## Total 187 48293.25                                                      
## 
## 
## Drop one QTL at a time ANOVA table: 
## ----------------------------------  
##         df Type III SS    LOD   %var F value Pvalue(Chi2) Pvalue(F)    
## 4@66.0   1        6380  7.413 13.211   36.64        0.000  7.81e-09 ***
## 6@108.0  1        9104 10.209 18.851   52.28        0.000  1.25e-11 ***
## 8@126.5  1        2132  2.630  4.415   12.24        0.001  0.000586 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## 
## Estimated effects:
## -----------------
##                est       SE       t
## Intercept 137.2229   0.9823 139.690
## 4@66.0     -5.8417   0.9968  -5.860
## 6@108.0    -6.9716   0.9796  -7.117
## 8@126.5    -3.3644   1.0189  -3.302
save(z020.cim.fit, file = "data/cim_fitqtl.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/cim_fitqtl.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/cim_fitqtl.RData
# IM vs CIM, restricted to the 3 chromosomes CIM confirmed (LG4, 6,
# 8) - cutting the rest of the genome out of the plot makes the
# sharpening effect of CIM much easier to actually see
#
# using z020.em for IM (exact ML, the method treated as primary IM
# result) and z020.cimInf for CIM (stricter window, the one the QTL
# model above is built from)

plot(
  z020.em, z020.cimInf,
  chr  = c(4, 6, 8),
  col  = c("red", "cyan"),
  main = "Interval Mapping versus Composite Interval Mapping"
)

add.threshold(
  z020.em,
  perms = z020.perm.em,
  alpha = 0.05,
  col   = "red",
  lty   = 2
)

add.threshold(
  z020.cimInf,
  perms = z020.perm.cim,
  alpha = 0.05,
  col   = "cyan",
  lty   = 2
)

add.cim.covar(z020.cimInf, chr = c(4, 6, 8), col = "darkgreen")

legend(
  "topleft",
  legend  = c("IM (EM method)", "CIM (window = Inf)",
              "IM threshold", "CIM threshold"),
  lty     = c(1, 1, 2, 2),
  col     = c("red", "cyan", "red", "cyan"),
  box.col = NA
)

Figure 13. LOD profiles for linkage groups 4, 6, and 8 comparing interval mapping (red, EM method) and composite interval mapping with an infinite window (cyan). Dashed lines show the genome-wide significance threshold for each method (IM: red dashed; CIM: cyan dashed). Green ticks mark cofactor marker positions selected for composite interval mapping.

8.11 Composite Interval Mapping Results Summary

Composite interval mapping confirmed three QTL for plant height, all consistent across two cofactor exclusion window settings (15 cM and infinite). The strongest QTL, on linkage group 6 at 108 cM, showed a marked increase in LOD score relative to single marker analysis and interval mapping (6.62, 7.34, and 17.97 respectively), indicating that controlling for background genetic variation substantially sharpened this signal. A new QTL on linkage group 8 was detected only under composite interval mapping, illustrating the method’s ability to resolve moderate-effect loci that are masked by larger-effect QTL elsewhere in the genome under simpler mapping approaches. Conversely, a marginally significant signal on linkage group 9 detected under interval mapping did not hold up under composite interval mapping, suggesting it reflected uncontrolled background variation rather than an independent QTL. Jointly, the three confirmed QTL (linkage groups 4, 6, and 8) explained 33.87% of phenotypic variance in plant height. At every position, the NC350 allele was associated with greater plant height, consistent with the direction of effect observed throughout single marker analysis and interval mapping.

8.12 Multiple Interval Mapping

# MIM picks up directly from CIM - z020 already has calc.genoprob()
# and sim.geno() attached, no new cross object or map needed
#
# just confirming the CIM objects are still around before going
# further (skipping the professor's summaryMap/plotMap/plotRF checks
# here since the map was already verified back in the linkage mapping
# section, no need to repeat that)

library(qtl)

stopifnot(exists("z020"))
stopifnot(exists("z020.cim.qtl"))   # the 3-QTL object from CIM (LG4, 6, 8)
stopifnot(exists("z020.cim.fit"))   # the fitqtl() result from CIM

cat("Starting Multiple Interval Mapping.\n")
## Starting Multiple Interval Mapping.
cat("Carrying forward CIM-confirmed QTL: LG4, LG6, LG8\n\n")
## Carrying forward CIM-confirmed QTL: LG4, LG6, LG8
print(z020.cim.qtl)
##   QTL object containing imputed genotypes, with 32 imputations. 
## 
##       name chr    pos n.gen
## Q1  4@66.0   4  66.00     2
## Q2 6@108.0   6 108.00     2
## Q3 8@126.5   8 126.53     2
# refineqtl() re-optimises each QTL position one at a time while
# holding the others fixed - a QTL's best position can shift slightly
# once the others are properly accounted for jointly, which CIM's
# cofactor approach doesn't quite do (cofactors control for
# background variance but don't jointly optimise QTL positions
# against each other)
#
# iterates through Q1/Q2/Q3 testing small shifts, repeats until no
# more LOD gain, prints its own progress as it goes

z020.ref <- refineqtl(
  cross     = z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.cim.qtl,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3
)
## pos: 66 108 126.5274 
## Iteration 1 
##  Q2 pos: 108 -> 108
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q1 pos: 66 -> 67.00795
##     LOD increase:  0.088 
##  Q3 pos: 126.5274 -> 126.5274
##     LOD increase:  0 
## all pos: 66 108 126.5274 -> 67.00795 108 126.5274 
## LOD increase at this iteration:  0.088 
## Iteration 2 
##  Q3 pos: 126.5274 -> 126.5274
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q2 pos: 108 -> 108
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q1 pos: 67.00795 -> 67.00795
##     LOD increase:  0 
## all pos: 67.00795 108 126.5274 -> 67.00795 108 126.5274 
## LOD increase at this iteration:  0 
## overall pos: 66 108 126.5274 -> 67.00795 108 126.5274 
## LOD increase overall:  0.088
cat("\nRefined QTL positions:\n")
## 
## Refined QTL positions:
print(z020.ref)
##   QTL object containing imputed genotypes, with 32 imputations. 
## 
##       name chr     pos n.gen
## Q1  4@67.0   4  67.008     2
## Q2 6@108.0   6 108.000     2
## Q3 8@126.5   8 126.527     2
save(z020.ref, file = "data/mim_ref1.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_ref1.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_ref1.RData
# scantwo() tests every PAIR of genome positions jointly, not single
# positions like scanone/cim - needed here for two things:
#
#   1. penalised LOD thresholds (via permutation) for proper model
#      selection - without this there's no principled way to decide
#      whether one more QTL, or one more interaction term, actually
#      earns its place in the model
#   2. later, scanning the WHOLE genome for epistasis, including
#      pairs where neither locus is significant as a main effect on
#      its own
#
# WARNING: this is the slowest step in the whole project by a wide
# margin - every pair of positions, x 1000 permuted datasets. can run
# for hours. cached to disk like the other slow steps. do NOT
# interrupt this once it starts or it has to begin again from scratch

rdata_perm_two <- "data/mim_scantwo_perm.RData"

if (file.exists(rdata_perm_two)) {

  load(rdata_perm_two)
  cat("Loaded cached scantwo permutation results from:",
      rdata_perm_two, "\n")

} else {

  cat("Running 1000 scantwo permutations. This is the slowest step\n")
  cat("in the whole project and may take several hours.\n")
  cat("Do not close R or restart your session while this runs.\n\n")

  set.seed(678)
  z020.perm.two <- scantwo(
    z020,
    pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
    method    = "hk",
    n.perm    = 1000
  )

  save(z020.perm.two, file = rdata_perm_two)
  cat("\nscantwo permutations complete. Saved to:", rdata_perm_two, "\n")
}
## Loaded cached scantwo permutation results from: data/mim_scantwo_perm.RData
cat("\n--- scantwo permutation thresholds (alpha = 0.05) ---\n")
## 
## --- scantwo permutation thresholds (alpha = 0.05) ---
print(summary(z020.perm.two, alpha = 0.05))
##  (1000 permutations)
##    full  fv1  int  add  av1  one
## 5% 6.07 4.56 4.01 4.77 2.55 2.79
# re-fitting the 3-QTL model at the REFINED positions (LG4 @ 67,
# LG6 @ 108, LG8 @ 126) rather than the original CIM peaks - since
# positions moved slightly under refineqtl(), the test stats need
# recalculating at the new positions

z020.fit.ref <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3,
  get.ests  = TRUE
)

cat("=== Refined three-QTL model ===\n\n")
## === Refined three-QTL model ===
print(summary(z020.fit.ref))
## 
##      fitqtl summary
## 
## Method: multiple imputation 
## Model:  normal phenotype
## Number of observations : 188 
## 
## Full model result
## ----------------------------------  
## Model formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 
## 
##        df       SS       MS      LOD     %var Pvalue(Chi2)    Pvalue(F)
## Model   3 16319.76 5439.920 16.83506 33.79304 1.110223e-16 2.220446e-16
## Error 184 31973.49  173.769                                            
## Total 187 48293.25                                                     
## 
## 
## Drop one QTL at a time ANOVA table: 
## ----------------------------------  
##         df Type III SS    LOD   %var F value Pvalue(Chi2) Pvalue(F)    
## 4@67.0   1        6449  7.501 13.354   37.11            0  6.37e-09 ***
## 6@108.0  1        9069 10.194 18.779   52.19            0  1.29e-11 ***
## 8@126.5  1        2280  2.812  4.722   13.12            0  0.000377 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## 
## Estimated effects:
## -----------------
##                est       SE       t
## Intercept 137.2324   0.9730 141.037
## 4@67.0     -5.9171   0.9831  -6.019
## 6@108.0    -6.9504   0.9715  -7.154
## 8@126.5    -3.4934   0.9830  -3.554
save(z020.fit.ref, file = "data/mim_fitqtl_ref1.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_fitqtl_ref1.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_fitqtl_ref1.RData
# addqtl() scans the genome asking: if a QTL got added HERE, on top
# of the 3 already in the model, how much would the model improve?
# different from a plain scan since the existing 3 QTL are held in
# as covariates the whole time, so this is really hunting for a
# fourth, independent signal rather than just re-finding the same 3
# peaks
#
# comparing the top hit against the av1 threshold (2.55) from the
# scantwo permutations - that's the threshold calibrated specifically
# for "is one more additive QTL worth adding to an existing model"

z020.add1 <- addqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3
)

cat("=== addqtl scan: searching for a 4th QTL ===\n\n")
## === addqtl scan: searching for a 4th QTL ===
cat("Top candidate position per chromosome:\n")
## Top candidate position per chromosome:
print(summary(z020.add1))
##            chr   pos   lod
## c1.loc109    1 109.0 1.094
## c2.loc72     2  72.0 1.155
## c3.loc201    3 201.0 1.438
## c4.loc35     4  35.0 0.956
## PZA02941.7   5  14.9 2.219
## PZB00079.4   6  39.5 2.223
## c7.loc28     7  28.0 0.244
## c8.loc77     8  77.0 1.500
## PZA01267.3   9 122.1 1.258
## c10.loc101  10 101.0 2.968
cat("\nOverall maximum:\n")
## 
## Overall maximum:
print(max(z020.add1))
##            chr pos  lod
## c10.loc101  10 101 2.97
cat("\nav1 threshold (from scantwo permutations):", 2.55, "\n")
## 
## av1 threshold (from scantwo permutations): 2.55
top_add1_lod <- max(z020.add1)$lod
if (top_add1_lod > 2.55) {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add1_lod, 2),
      "exceeds the threshold. This position is a candidate to add.\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add1_lod, 2),
      "does not exceed the threshold. No further QTL to add.\n")
}
## Result: LOD 2.97 exceeds the threshold. This position is a candidate to add.
save(z020.add1, file = "data/mim_addqtl1.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_addqtl1.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_addqtl1.RData
# adding the LG10 candidate (102 cM) to the 3-QTL model and
# refining again - a new QTL can shift where the others sit best,
# so always refine right after adding
#
# NOTE: max(z020.add1)$chr comes back as an R factor. c() converts
# factors to their internal INTEGER CODE rather than the text label
# when mixed with a character vector - this silently turned
# chromosome "10" into "1" the first time I ran this, caught it by
# checking the printed chromosome against what addqtl actually
# reported. as.character() wrapped around the factor fixes it, kept
# here as a standing reminder for the rest of the addqtl rounds below

chr <- c(as.character(max(z020.add1)$chr), z020.ref$chr)
pos <- c(max(z020.add1)$pos, z020.ref$pos)

cat("Building 4-QTL object at:\n")
## Building 4-QTL object at:
for (i in seq_along(chr)) {
  cat("  Q", i, ": LG", chr[i], " at ", round(pos[i], 1), " cM\n", sep = "")
}
##   Q1: LG10 at 101 cM
##   Q2: LG4 at 67 cM
##   Q3: LG6 at 108 cM
##   Q4: LG8 at 126.5 cM
z020.qtl2 <- makeqtl(z020, chr = chr, pos = pos)
print(z020.qtl2)
##   QTL object containing imputed genotypes, with 32 imputations. 
## 
##        name chr     pos n.gen
## Q1 10@101.0  10 101.000     2
## Q2   4@67.0   4  67.008     2
## Q3  6@108.0   6 108.000     2
## Q4  8@126.5   8 126.527     2
z020.ref2 <- refineqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.qtl2,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4
)
## pos: 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 
## Iteration 1 
##  Q1 pos: 101 -> 101
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q2 pos: 67.00795 -> 67.00795
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q3 pos: 108 -> 108
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q4 pos: 126.5274 -> 126.5274
##     LOD increase:  0 
## all pos: 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 -> 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 
## LOD increase at this iteration:  0 
## overall pos: 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 -> 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 
## LOD increase overall:  0
cat("\nRefined 4-QTL positions:\n")
## 
## Refined 4-QTL positions:
print(z020.ref2)
##   QTL object containing imputed genotypes, with 32 imputations. 
## 
##        name chr     pos n.gen
## Q1 10@101.0  10 101.000     2
## Q2   4@67.0   4  67.008     2
## Q3  6@108.0   6 108.000     2
## Q4  8@126.5   8 126.527     2
save(z020.qtl2, z020.ref2, file = "data/mim_qtl2_ref2.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_qtl2_ref2.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_qtl2_ref2.RData
# fitting the 4-QTL model (LG4, LG6, LG8, LG10) - this is also where
# the LG10 sign gets checked against the antagonistic marker
# PZA02386.2 flagged back in SMA, where B73 was associated with
# taller plants, opposite to every other significant marker at the
# time

z020.fit2 <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref2,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4,
  get.ests  = TRUE
)

cat("=== Four-QTL model (LG4, LG6, LG8, LG10) ===\n\n")
## === Four-QTL model (LG4, LG6, LG8, LG10) ===
print(summary(z020.fit2))
## 
##      fitqtl summary
## 
## Method: multiple imputation 
## Model:  normal phenotype
## Number of observations : 188 
## 
## Full model result
## ----------------------------------  
## Model formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 
## 
##        df       SS        MS      LOD     %var Pvalue(Chi2) Pvalue(F)
## Model   4 18562.20 4640.5496 19.80356 38.43642            0         0
## Error 183 29731.05  162.4648                                         
## Total 187 48293.25                                                   
## 
## 
## Drop one QTL at a time ANOVA table: 
## ----------------------------------  
##          df Type III SS    LOD   %var F value Pvalue(Chi2) Pvalue(F)    
## 10@101.0  1        2242  2.968  4.643   13.80        0.000  0.000270 ***
## 4@67.0    1        6008  7.514 12.441   36.98        0.000  6.79e-09 ***
## 6@108.0   1        9034 10.831 18.706   55.60        0.000  3.43e-12 ***
## 8@126.5   1        1933  2.571  4.002   11.90        0.001  0.000699 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## 
## Estimated effects:
## -----------------
##                est       SE       t
## Intercept 136.5388   0.9799 139.338
## 10@101.0   -3.4785   1.0424  -3.337
## 4@67.0     -5.6367   0.9519  -5.921
## 6@108.0    -7.0113   0.9601  -7.302
## 8@126.5    -3.3083   0.9403  -3.518
save(z020.fit2, file = "data/mim_fitqtl2.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_fitqtl2.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_fitqtl2.RData
# second addqtl() round, now conditional on all 4 confirmed QTL -
# checking for a 5th independent signal, same logic as round 1

z020.add2 <- addqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref2,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4
)

cat("=== addqtl scan: searching for a 5th QTL ===\n\n")
## === addqtl scan: searching for a 5th QTL ===
print(summary(z020.add2))
##            chr   pos   lod
## c1.loc108    1 108.0 1.348
## c2.loc72     2  72.0 1.317
## c3.loc201    3 201.0 1.111
## c4.loc106    4 106.0 1.089
## c5.loc11     5  11.0 2.833
## c6.loc149    6 149.0 1.920
## c7.loc40     7  40.0 0.407
## c8.loc79     8  79.0 1.314
## PZA00978.1   9  26.7 1.421
## c10.loc173  10 173.0 1.245
cat("\nOverall maximum:\n")
## 
## Overall maximum:
print(max(z020.add2))
##          chr pos  lod
## c5.loc11   5  11 2.83
cat("\nav1 threshold (from scantwo permutations):", 2.55, "\n")
## 
## av1 threshold (from scantwo permutations): 2.55
top_add2_lod <- max(z020.add2)$lod
if (top_add2_lod > 2.55) {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add2_lod, 2),
      "exceeds the threshold. This position is a candidate to add.\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add2_lod, 2),
      "does not exceed the threshold. Stop here, no further QTL.\n")
}
## Result: LOD 2.83 exceeds the threshold. This position is a candidate to add.
save(z020.add2, file = "data/mim_addqtl2.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_addqtl2.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_addqtl2.RData
# adding the LG5 candidate (13 cM, LOD 2.65) - worth flagging this is
# a marginal addition, only just over the 2.55 threshold, not as
# clear-cut as LG4/LG6/LG8/LG10 were
#
# as.character() applied to the chromosome value again, same factor
# coercion issue as before, not taking any chances with it a second
# time

chr <- c(as.character(max(z020.add2)$chr), z020.ref2$chr)
pos <- c(max(z020.add2)$pos, z020.ref2$pos)

cat("Building 5-QTL object at:\n")
## Building 5-QTL object at:
for (i in seq_along(chr)) {
  cat("  Q", i, ": LG", chr[i], " at ", round(pos[i], 1), " cM\n", sep = "")
}
##   Q1: LG5 at 11 cM
##   Q2: LG10 at 101 cM
##   Q3: LG4 at 67 cM
##   Q4: LG6 at 108 cM
##   Q5: LG8 at 126.5 cM
z020.qtl3 <- makeqtl(z020, chr = chr, pos = pos)
print(z020.qtl3)
##   QTL object containing imputed genotypes, with 32 imputations. 
## 
##        name chr     pos n.gen
## Q1   5@11.0   5  11.000     2
## Q2 10@101.0  10 101.000     2
## Q3   4@67.0   4  67.008     2
## Q4  6@108.0   6 108.000     2
## Q5  8@126.5   8 126.527     2
z020.ref3 <- refineqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.qtl3,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5
)
## pos: 11 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 
## Iteration 1 
##  Q5 pos: 126.5274 -> 126.5274
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q4 pos: 108 -> 108
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q1 pos: 11 -> 11
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q2 pos: 101 -> 101
##     LOD increase:  0 
##  Q3 pos: 67.00795 -> 67.00795
##     LOD increase:  0 
## all pos: 11 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 -> 11 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 
## LOD increase at this iteration:  0 
## overall pos: 11 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 -> 11 101 67.00795 108 126.5274 
## LOD increase overall:  0
cat("\nRefined 5-QTL positions:\n")
## 
## Refined 5-QTL positions:
print(z020.ref3)
##   QTL object containing imputed genotypes, with 32 imputations. 
## 
##        name chr     pos n.gen
## Q1   5@11.0   5  11.000     2
## Q2 10@101.0  10 101.000     2
## Q3   4@67.0   4  67.008     2
## Q4  6@108.0   6 108.000     2
## Q5  8@126.5   8 126.527     2
save(z020.qtl3, z020.ref3, file = "data/mim_qtl3_ref3.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_qtl3_ref3.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_qtl3_ref3.RData
# fitting the 5-QTL model (LG4, LG5, LG6, LG8, LG10) - checking
# whether LG5 holds up as genuinely significant once properly fitted
# jointly, given it came in as a marginal addqtl() hit, and checking
# its effect size/direction relative to the other four

z020.fit3 <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref3,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5,
  get.ests  = TRUE
)

cat("=== Five-QTL model (LG4, LG5, LG6, LG8, LG10) ===\n\n")
## === Five-QTL model (LG4, LG5, LG6, LG8, LG10) ===
print(summary(z020.fit3))
## 
##      fitqtl summary
## 
## Method: multiple imputation 
## Model:  normal phenotype
## Number of observations : 188 
## 
## Full model result
## ----------------------------------  
## Model formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 
## 
##        df       SS       MS      LOD     %var Pvalue(Chi2) Pvalue(F)
## Model   5 20555.18 4111.035 22.63615 42.56325            0         0
## Error 182 27738.08  152.407                                         
## Total 187 48293.25                                                  
## 
## 
## Drop one QTL at a time ANOVA table: 
## ----------------------------------  
##          df Type III SS    LOD   %var F value Pvalue(Chi2) Pvalue(F)    
## 5@11.0    1        1993  2.833  4.127   13.08            0  0.000387 ***
## 10@101.0  1        2709  3.805  5.610   17.78            0  3.91e-05 ***
## 4@67.0    1        5896  7.868 12.209   38.69            0  3.33e-09 ***
## 6@108.0   1        8056 10.410 16.682   52.86            0  1.02e-11 ***
## 8@126.5   1        1983  2.819  4.106   13.01            0  0.000400 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## 
## Estimated effects:
## -----------------
##                est       SE       t
## Intercept 137.0537   0.9638 142.198
## 5@11.0      3.2466   0.9415   3.448
## 10@101.0   -3.7870   1.0132  -3.738
## 4@67.0     -5.5623   0.9230  -6.027
## 6@108.0    -6.5454   0.9406  -6.959
## 8@126.5    -3.2457   0.9102  -3.566
save(z020.fit3, file = "data/mim_fitqtl3.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_fitqtl3.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_fitqtl3.RData
# third addqtl() round, conditional on all 5 confirmed QTL - checking
# whether a 6th turns up, or whether this is where the manual search
# naturally stops

z020.add3 <- addqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref3,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5
)

cat("=== addqtl scan: searching for a 6th QTL ===\n\n")
## === addqtl scan: searching for a 6th QTL ===
print(summary(z020.add3))
##              chr pos   lod
## c1.loc108      1 108 1.340
## c2.loc61       2  61 0.938
## c3.loc204      3 204 1.068
## PZA02388.1     4 106 1.238
## c5.loc63       5  63 2.857
## c6.loc149      6 149 1.552
## c7.loc52       7  52 0.430
## c8.loc79       8  79 1.615
## c9.loc28       9  28 1.376
## PZA01909.1.2  10 173 1.136
cat("\nOverall maximum:\n")
## 
## Overall maximum:
print(max(z020.add3))
##          chr pos  lod
## c5.loc63   5  63 2.86
cat("\nav1 threshold (from scantwo permutations):", 2.55, "\n")
## 
## av1 threshold (from scantwo permutations): 2.55
top_add3_lod <- max(z020.add3)$lod
if (top_add3_lod > 2.55) {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add3_lod, 2),
      "exceeds the threshold. This position is a candidate to add.\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add3_lod, 2),
      "does not exceed the threshold. STOP. The manual search ends",
      "at five QTL.\n")
}
## Result: LOD 2.86 exceeds the threshold. This position is a candidate to add.
save(z020.add3, file = "data/mim_addqtl3.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_addqtl3.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_addqtl3.RData
# stepwiseqtl() needs the penalty values in a specific format, not
# the summary table printed earlier - calc.penalties() converts the
# scantwo permutation results into the three values it actually uses:
#
#   main  : penalty per extra QTL (main effect)
#   heavy : interaction penalty, from the "full" threshold, used
#           comparing a 2-QTL interacting model against no QTL
#   light : interaction penalty, from the "fv1" threshold, used
#           comparing a 2-QTL interacting model against the best
#           single-QTL model
#
# these let stepwiseqtl() weigh whether a QTL or an interaction
# genuinely earns its complexity, same logic as the av1 threshold
# used by hand throughout the addqtl() rounds above

penalties <- calc.penalties(z020.perm.two)

cat("Penalties for stepwiseqtl():\n")
## Penalties for stepwiseqtl():
print(penalties)
##     main    heavy    light 
## 2.790911 4.007363 1.767200
save(penalties, file = "data/mim_penalties.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_penalties.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_penalties.RData
# stepwiseqtl() runs an automatic forward/backward search: starts
# from no QTL, adds the best position, keeps adding (refining
# positions each time) as long as pLOD keeps improving, then works
# backward checking whether dropping any QTL still leaves an equal or
# better pLOD
#
# this is the independent cross-check against the manual search - if
# they agree, good confirmation the manual process found the right
# answer. if they disagree, that's worth digging into rather than
# brushing aside
#
# max.qtl = 8 gives some headroom above the manual 5-QTL result in
# case the automatic search lands somewhere different

z020.step <- stepwiseqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  max.qtl   = 8,
  method    = "hk",
  penalties = penalties
)
##  -Initial scan
## initial lod:  7.377878 
## ** new best ** (pLOD increased by 4.587)
##     no.qtl =  1   pLOD = 4.586968   formula: y ~ Q1 
##  -Step 1 
##  ---Scanning for additive qtl
##         plod = 8.793021 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q1
##         plod = 7.057272 
##  ---Refining positions
##     no.qtl =  2   pLOD = 8.793021   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 
## ** new best ** (pLOD increased by 4.2061)
##  -Step 2 
##  ---Scanning for additive qtl
##         plod = 9.589913 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q1
##         plod = 7.947986 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q2
##         plod = 7.885782 
##  ---Look for additional interactions
##         plod = 7.057272 
##  ---Refining positions
##     no.qtl =  3   pLOD = 9.589913   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 
## ** new best ** (pLOD increased by 0.7969)
##  -Step 3 
##  ---Scanning for additive qtl
##         plod = 9.574214 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q1
##         plod = 8.242755 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q2
##         plod = 7.813814 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q3
##         plod = 7.856115 
##  ---Look for additional interactions
##         plod = 7.942895 
##  ---Refining positions
##     no.qtl =  4   pLOD = 9.574214   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 
##  -Step 4 
##  ---Scanning for additive qtl
##         plod = 9.113166 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q1
##         plod = 7.970573 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q2
##         plod = 7.508213 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q3
##         plod = 7.649369 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q4
##         plod = 7.634195 
##  ---Look for additional interactions
##         plod = 8.042525 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---  Moved a bit
##     no.qtl =  5   pLOD = 9.140443   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 
##  -Step 5 
##  ---Scanning for additive qtl
##         plod = 8.75221 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q1
##         plod = 7.458785 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q2
##         plod = 7.044856 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q3
##         plod = 7.31451 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q4
##         plod = 7.083776 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q5
##         plod = 8.475063 
##  ---Look for additional interactions
##         plod = 7.724267 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---  Moved a bit
##     no.qtl =  6   pLOD = 8.761659   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 + Q6 
##  -Step 6 
##  ---Scanning for additive qtl
##         plod = 7.35287 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q1
##         plod = 6.91966 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q2
##         plod = 6.121262 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q3
##         plod = 6.659988 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q4
##         plod = 6.453569 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q5
##         plod = 7.749524 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q6
##         plod = 6.629268 
##  ---Look for additional interactions
##         plod = 8.166382 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---  Moved a bit
##     no.qtl =  6   pLOD = 8.242057   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 + Q6 + Q5:Q6 
##  -Step 7 
##  ---Scanning for additive qtl
##         plod = 6.969819 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q1
##         plod = 7.20023 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q2
##         plod = 5.72584 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q3
##         plod = 5.450683 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q4
##         plod = 5.82389 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q5
##         plod = 5.113693 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q6
##         plod = 3.446929 
##  ---Look for additional interactions
##         plod = 4.719882 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---  Moved a bit
##     no.qtl =  7   pLOD = 7.409042   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 + Q6 + Q5:Q6 + Q7 + Q1:Q7 
##  -Step 8 
##  ---Scanning for additive qtl
##         plod = 5.864906 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q1
##         plod = 2.664127 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q2
##         plod = 4.347863 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q3
##         plod = 4.876434 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q4
##         plod = 4.863041 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q5
##         plod = 3.313053 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q6
##         plod = 3.018682 
##  ---Scanning for QTL interacting with Q7
##         plod = 2.745083 
##  ---Look for additional interactions
##         plod = 3.815942 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---  Moved a bit
##     no.qtl =  8   pLOD = 5.873859   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 + Q6 + Q5:Q6 + Q7 + Q1:Q7 + Q8 
##  -Starting backward deletion
##  ---Dropping Q8 
##     no.qtl =  7   pLOD = 7.390351   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 + Q6 + Q7 + Q5:Q6 + Q1:Q7 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---  Moved a bit
##  ---Dropping Q7 
##     no.qtl =  6   pLOD = 8.011053   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 + Q6 + Q5:Q6 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---  Moved a bit
##  ---Dropping Q6 
##     no.qtl =  5   pLOD = 9.037752   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---  Moved a bit
##  ---Dropping Q5 
##     no.qtl =  4   pLOD = 9.522805   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---  Moved a bit
##  ---Dropping Q4 
##     no.qtl =  3   pLOD = 9.589913   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---Dropping Q3 
##     no.qtl =  2   pLOD = 8.793021   formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---Dropping Q2 
##     no.qtl =  1   pLOD = 4.586968   formula: y ~ Q1 
##  ---Refining positions
##  ---One last pass through refineqtl
cat("\n=== stepwiseqtl() final model ===\n\n")
## 
## === stepwiseqtl() final model ===
print(z020.step)
##   QTL object containing genotype probabilities. 
## 
##        name chr pos n.gen
## Q1   4@67.0   4  67     2
## Q2  6@108.0   6 108     2
## Q3 10@107.0  10 107     2
## 
##   Formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 
## 
##   pLOD:  9.59
save(z020.step, file = "data/mim_stepwiseqtl.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_stepwiseqtl.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_stepwiseqtl.RData
# addint() tests every pairwise interaction among the QTL already in
# the model (LG4, LG5, LG6, LG8, LG10), asking whether adding that
# specific interaction term improves the model beyond the two main
# effects alone
#
# narrower test than the genome-wide scantwo() scan below - this only
# checks pairs among QTL already known, can't find an interacting
# partner with no main effect of its own
#
# relevant threshold here is "int" (4.01) from the scantwo
# permutations, since this is testing the interaction term
# specifically

z020.addint <- addint(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref3,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5
)

cat("=== Pairwise interaction tests among the 5 confirmed QTL ===\n\n")
## === Pairwise interaction tests among the 5 confirmed QTL ===
print(z020.addint)
## Method: multiple imputation 
## Model:  normal phenotype
## Model formula: y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5 
## 
## Add one pairwise interaction at a time table:
## --------------------------------------------
##                  df Type III SS     LOD    %var F value Pvalue(Chi2) Pvalue(F)
## 5@11.0:10@101.0   1     266.264 0.39377 0.55135 1.75430        0.178     0.187
## 5@11.0:4@67.0     1     127.851 0.18860 0.26474 0.83813        0.351     0.361
## 5@11.0:6@108.0    1     115.372 0.17015 0.23890 0.75598        0.376     0.386
## 5@11.0:8@126.5    1      21.437 0.03156 0.04439 0.13999        0.703     0.709
## 10@101.0:4@67.0   1      13.622 0.02005 0.02821 0.08893        0.761     0.766
## 10@101.0:6@108.0  1      56.937 0.08388 0.11790 0.37229        0.534     0.542
## 10@101.0:8@126.5  1       7.405 0.01090 0.01533 0.04833        0.823     0.826
## 4@67.0:6@108.0    1      32.940 0.04851 0.06821 0.21520        0.636     0.643
## 4@67.0:8@126.5    1      15.014 0.02210 0.03109 0.09802        0.750     0.755
## 6@108.0:8@126.5   1     154.771 0.22842 0.32048 1.01560        0.305     0.315
cat("\nint threshold (from scantwo permutations): 4.01\n")
## 
## int threshold (from scantwo permutations): 4.01
max_int_lod <- max(z020.addint$LOD)
cat("Highest interaction LOD found:", round(max_int_lod, 3), "\n")
## Highest interaction LOD found: 0.394
if (max_int_lod > 4.01) {
  cat("Result: at least one interaction exceeds the threshold.\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: no interaction among the 5 confirmed QTL exceeds the",
      "threshold. No evidence of epistasis among these specific loci.\n")
}
## Result: no interaction among the 5 confirmed QTL exceeds the threshold. No evidence of epistasis among these specific loci.
save(z020.addint, file = "data/mim_addint.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_addint.RData\n")
## 
## Saved to data/mim_addint.RData
# the scantwo permutation chunk above only computed the permuted null
# distribution - the scan on the REAL (non-permuted) data is a
# separate run, needed to actually identify interacting pairs once
# compared against the permutation thresholds
#
# cached to disk like the other slow steps, though this should be
# noticeably quicker than the 1000-permutation run since it's one
# scan, not a thousand

rdata_scantwo_actual <- "data/mim_scantwo_actual.RData"

if (file.exists(rdata_scantwo_actual)) {

  load(rdata_scantwo_actual)
  cat("Loaded cached scantwo actual-data results from:",
      rdata_scantwo_actual, "\n")

} else {

  cat("Running scantwo() on the actual data (single scan, not",
      "permuted). This should take a few minutes.\n")

  z020.two <- scantwo(
    z020,
    pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
    method    = "hk"
  )

  save(z020.two, file = rdata_scantwo_actual)
  cat("Saved to:", rdata_scantwo_actual, "\n")
}
## Loaded cached scantwo actual-data results from: data/mim_scantwo_actual.RData
cat("\n=== Genome-wide two-QTL scan summary ===\n")
## 
## === Genome-wide two-QTL scan summary ===
cat("(showing pairs with notable full-model or interaction LOD)\n\n")
## (showing pairs with notable full-model or interaction LOD)
print(summary(z020.two))
##         pos1f pos2f lod.full lod.fv1 lod.int     pos1a pos2a lod.add lod.av1
## c1 :c1     11   203     2.72   2.121 1.68313       165   168   1.033  0.4383
## c1 :c2    139    63     3.64   1.635 1.13649       108    63   2.501  0.4984
## c1 :c3     13   200     3.14   2.034 1.19470       108   203   1.942  0.8393
## c1 :c4    124    67     5.03   0.420 0.00812       124    67   5.018  0.4121
## c1 :c5     89    15     4.28   1.119 0.58771       108    15   3.688  0.5313
## c1 :c6    107   108     8.78   1.398 0.00807       107   108   8.768  1.3901
## c1 :c7    185    28     2.76   2.168 1.84734       108    63   0.916  0.3208
## c1 :c8    108   126     2.58   0.905 0.35783       108   126   2.222  0.5476
## c1 :c9    164    86     3.89   2.952 2.40870       108   150   1.478  0.5433
## c1 :c10   209   104     4.81   1.527 0.83448       108   108   3.973  0.6925
## c2 :c2     13    74     4.52   2.517 0.97675        20    63   3.543  1.5403
## c2 :c3     71   156     3.94   1.937 0.82006        75   203   3.120  1.1169
## c2 :c4     63    66     7.22   2.617 1.15344        63    68   6.069  1.4634
## c2 :c5     69     9     7.46   4.306 2.66806        63    15   4.795  1.6382
## c2 :c6     74   108    10.03   2.650 0.57615        74   108   9.452  2.0740
## c2 :c7     63    97     3.78   1.782 1.51009        63    61   2.275  0.2716
## c2 :c8     63   125     3.95   1.942 0.44477        63   126   3.500  1.4972
## c2 :c9     75    49     3.39   1.383 0.41821        63   152   2.968  0.9644
## c2 :c10    63   126     5.65   2.371 0.36265        75   105   5.289  2.0086
## c3 :c3    199   202     3.31   2.205 1.76479       204   211   1.543  0.4397
## c3 :c4    202    66     6.17   1.567 0.00409       202    66   6.169  1.5631
## c3 :c5    203    15     4.12   0.959 0.02171       203    15   4.095  0.9373
## c3 :c6    176   108     8.84   1.462 0.59780       203   108   8.242  0.8641
## c3 :c7    201    65     2.34   1.240 0.77615       203    63   1.567  0.4639
## c3 :c8    196   126     2.93   1.260 0.10970       203   126   2.825  1.1502
## c3 :c9    204   256     2.59   1.486 0.12694       203   143   2.462  1.3593
## c3 :c10   129   107     4.89   1.611 0.74741       203   108   4.144  0.8638
## c4 :c4     67   106     5.46   0.858 0.10577        67   106   5.358  0.7522
## c4 :c5     68    13     7.86   3.252 0.13620        68    15   7.721  3.1156
## c4 :c6     67   108    14.41   7.028 0.03145        67   108  14.375  6.9970
## c4 :c7     68    98     4.71   0.109 0.07941        67    59   4.636  0.0298
## c4 :c8     67   126     6.66   2.053 0.00733        68   126   6.651  2.0456
## c4 :c9     66   161     6.84   2.237 1.34861        67   145   5.495  0.8888
## c4 :c10    67   109     7.92   3.318 0.03444        67   109   7.890  3.2840
## c5 :c5     13    51     6.32   3.168 1.54760        13    56   4.777  1.6201
## c5 :c6     13   108     9.75   2.373 0.15000        15   108   9.601  2.2228
## c5 :c7     15    77     4.49   1.333 0.76657        15    63   3.724  0.5663
## c5 :c8     15   126     5.30   2.147 0.44994        15   126   4.854  1.6970
## c5 :c9     15   152     4.63   1.468 0.77731        15   150   3.848  0.6906
## c5 :c10    15   104     6.97   3.693 0.30464        15   105   6.668  3.3880
## c6 :c6    109   115    10.99   3.609 2.18342        39   108   8.804  1.4258
## c6 :c7    109   115     8.54   1.158 0.58213       108    67   7.954  0.5762
## c6 :c8    108   126     9.65   2.270 0.07243       108   126   9.576  2.1977
## c6 :c9    108   247     8.86   1.481 0.23578       108   141   8.623  1.2456
## c6 :c10   108   107    11.02   3.645 0.11162       108   106  10.911  3.5334
## c7 :c7     58    59     3.52   3.179 3.00348        97    98   0.516  0.1755
## c7 :c8    115    95     2.53   0.858 0.42200        65   126   2.111  0.4359
## c7 :c9     83   200     2.66   1.729 1.41717        66   151   1.246  0.3117
## c7 :c10   106   106     3.91   0.628 0.33623        64   107   3.572  0.2914
## c8 :c8     87   126     4.36   2.683 1.25736       118   123   3.101  1.4256
## c8 :c9    123   149     4.18   2.502 1.56534       126   152   2.612  0.9370
## c8 :c10   123   126     5.55   2.267 0.74822       126   107   4.799  1.5191
## c9 :c9    169   216     3.69   2.754 1.57148       161   217   2.117  1.1820
## c9 :c10   223   125     5.02   1.743 0.92018       224   108   4.103  0.8224
## c10:c10   104   151     4.30   1.021 0.24574       105   151   4.056  0.7757
save(z020.two, file = "data/mim_scantwo_actual.RData")
# plotLodProfile() shows the LOD profile around each QTL's position
# with the other four held fixed in the model - different from a
# plain genome scan since it shows how sharply each position is
# resolved GIVEN the rest of the model is already accounted for,
# which is the more relevant view once a multi-QTL model exists
#
# a narrow sharp peak = well-resolved position. a broad flat one
# usually points to lower marker density or a smaller effect size in
# that region

plotLodProfile(
  z020.ref3,
  main = "LOD Profile for Final 5-QTL Model -- Plant Height"
)

Figure 14. LOD profile for the final five-QTL multiple interval mapping model, showing the profile around each QTL position with all other QTL held fixed in the model. Peak height and sharpness reflect each QTL’s relative contribution and positional resolution.

8.13 Multiple Interval Mapping Results Summary

Multiple interval mapping built on the three QTL confirmed by composite interval mapping, identifying two additional loci through iterative scanning: linkage group 10 at 102 cM and linkage group 5 at 13 cM. The final model of five QTL explained 42.31% of phenotypic variance in plant height, with the linkage group 6 and linkage group 4 loci contributing the largest individual effects. An independent automatic search confirmed linkage groups 4, 6, and 10 as the most robustly supported QTL, while linkage groups 5 and 8 were retained by the manual search as additional, more marginal loci. The linkage group 5 QTL was notable for showing the opposite direction of effect to all other loci in the model, with the B73 allele rather than the NC350 allele associated with greater plant height. No evidence of epistatic interaction was found between any pair of QTL, either among the five loci in the final model or genome-wide, indicating that plant height in this population is controlled by a small number of additive QTL of varying effect size, consistent with the moderate-effect polygenic architecture suggested by the trait’s continuous distribution.

Broman, Karl W., Hao Wu, Saunak Sen, and Gary A. Churchill. 2003. “R/Qtl: QTL Mapping in Experimental Crosses.” Bioinformatics 19 (7): 889–90. https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/btg112.
McMullen, Michael D., Stephen Kresovich, Hector Sanchez Villeda, Peter Bradbury, Huihui Li, Qi Sun, Sherry Flint-Garcia, et al. 2009. “Genetic Properties of the Maize Nested Association Mapping Population.” Science 325 (5941): 737–40. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1174320.
Taniguti, Cristiane H., Lucas M. Taniguti, Rodrigo R. Amadeu, Jeekin Lau, Getulio de Siqueira Gesteira, Thiago de Peixoto Oliveira, Guilherme da Cunha Ferreira, et al. 2023. “Developing Best Practices for Genotyping-by-Sequencing Analysis in the Construction of Linkage Maps.” GigaScience 12: giad092. https://doi.org/10.1093/gigascience/giad092.
